title: Preschool and Early Learning Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Preschool Business: Real Costs, Earnings, and Steps
metaDescription: Honest guide to opening a preschool or early learning center: real startup costs, licensing and ratio rules, realistic earnings, enrollment, and what most operators get wrong.
bestFor: People with early-childhood experience and patience for licensing who want to run a regulated, community-rooted education business
biggestRisk: Licensing delays or a serious safety incident, both can shut you down, and child injuries carry heavy liability
whatItIs: A preschool and early learning business is a licensed facility that provides care and structured early education to children, typically ages two to five, during working hours. Depending on your state and model, this can range from a small in-home licensed program of 6 to 12 children to a center-based school serving 40 to 100 or more. You charge tuition (weekly, monthly, or by the term), staff to legally mandated child-to-teacher ratios, and operate under heavy state regulation covering background checks, square footage, fencing, fire safety, nutrition, immunization records, and curriculum.
dailyReality: Your day runs around the children's day: arrivals and parent drop-off, structured activities and free play, meals and snacks, naptime, outdoor time, and pickup, all on a tight schedule. You are constantly counting heads, supervising staff, documenting incidents, communicating with parents, and handling the small crises of young children. Around that, expect ongoing administrative work, payroll, tuition billing, enrollment tours, licensing paperwork, and staff scheduling. As an owner-operator you often teach or fill in for absent staff, which means the business rarely lets you step away during operating hours.

startupCostsItemized.item: Facility lease deposit and first months (center model)
Buildout for code compliance (fencing, bathrooms, fire safety, ADA)
Licensing fees, background checks, and inspections
Furniture, cots, age-appropriate toys, and learning materials
Initial staff hiring, training, and first payroll cushion
Childcare management software (enrollment, billing, ratio tracking)
Marketing, signage, and a simple website

realEarnings.yearOne: Most new operators lose money or barely break even in year one while enrollment ramps and licensing finalizes. A small licensed in-home program can clear $2,000 to $5,000 per month to the owner once near capacity; a center often runs near break-even for 6 to 18 months as you fill seats.
realEarnings.experienced: An established, well-enrolled in-home program commonly nets the owner $4,000 to $9,000 per month. A center at strong occupancy (typically 80 percent or higher) can yield the owner $8,000 to $18,000 per month after rent, payroll, and insurance, but payroll is the dominant cost and ratios cap how lean you can run.
realEarnings.topEarners: Owners of multi-classroom centers at full enrollment, or operators of two to three locations, can clear $200,000 to $500,000-plus per year. Reaching that requires excellent directors, low staff turnover, strong waitlists, and often years of reinvestment. Many never get past one location because labor and regulation make scaling brutal.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Enrollment rate and staff ratios drive everything. Because you must staff to legal ratios whether a room is full or half-empty, the gap between 65 percent and 90 percent occupancy is the difference between losing money and a healthy margin. Staff turnover and unfilled spots quietly destroy profitability.
realEarnings.perHour: As an owner-operator working 45 to 60 hours a week through the ramp, your effective rate is often below $20 per hour for the first year or two. Established owners who have stepped into a director or oversight role can reach $40 to $80 per hour of their time.

howToStart: Months 1-2: Research your state's exact childcare licensing rules, they vary enormously. Decide between an in-home licensed program (far cheaper, fewer children) and a center. Confirm zoning, ratios, square-footage minimums, and what your space would need to pass inspection before you sign any lease.

Months 2-4: Secure a compliant location or prepare your home, complete background checks and required early-childhood training, and begin the licensing application. Budget for buildout, fencing, separate bathrooms, fire egress, and ADA access are common surprises that blow up early estimates.

Months 4-6: Hire and credential staff to meet ratios, set tuition based on local market rates and your true costs, choose childcare management software, and pass your final licensing inspection. Do not enroll children before your license is issued.

Months 6-9: Open with a soft launch and a few families, run enrollment tours, and build word of mouth. Track occupancy weekly. Get on local childcare referral lists and your state's subsidy provider list if you plan to accept assistance.

Months 9-18: Push toward 80 percent-plus occupancy, build a waitlist, and stabilize staffing. Only consider a second classroom or location once your first is reliably full and your director can run a day without you.

mustHave: Genuine experience with and patience for young children, plus the temperament to stay calm in chaos
Comfort navigating dense regulation, inspections, and meticulous record-keeping
Basic business and cash-flow management, payroll and rent come due whether rooms are full or not
canLearn: State-specific licensing procedures and how to pass inspections
Early-childhood curriculum frameworks and developmentally appropriate activity planning
Childcare management software for billing, attendance, and ratio tracking
separators: Hiring and retaining warm, reliable teachers in a high-turnover field, staffing quality is your reputation
Keeping occupancy high through reputation, waitlists, and low family churn
Building parent trust so safety incidents are handled transparently and families stay

whatPeopleGetWrong: Underestimating licensing timelines and buildout costs, then running out of cash before the doors open
Signing a lease before confirming the space can actually pass inspection for childcare use
Pricing tuition on competitors alone rather than on real staffing costs, leaving no margin once ratios are met
Opening below the occupancy needed to cover fixed payroll and assuming families will fill in fast, they rarely do
Skimping on staff pay and training, causing turnover that erodes parent trust and your reputation
Treating safety documentation and supervision as paperwork rather than the thing that keeps the business alive after an incident

toolsAndEquipment.note: Handles enrollment, tuition billing, attendance, and ratio tracking. Brightwheel and Procare are common choices.
Must meet safety standards; cribs and cots have specific code requirements.
Manipulatives, books, art supplies, and a chosen curriculum framework.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Outdoor play equipment and fenced area
toolsAndEquipment.note: Often the single biggest code expense; required in most jurisdictions.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Fire safety, first aid, and security systems
toolsAndEquipment.note: Extinguishers, alarms, secure entry, and door access controls.
Ongoing cost; sanitation standards are strictly inspected.

methods: Your state's childcare resource and referral network, which parents actively use to find licensed care
Google Business Profile with photos and reviews, parents search 'preschool near me' heavily
Local parent Facebook groups, Nextdoor, and word of mouth among families who already use you
Relationships with pediatricians, churches, and local employers who refer working parents
Open-house tours and a clear waitlist process that converts interested parents into committed enrollments
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Working parents of children roughly ages two to five within a short drive of your location, early learning is intensely local, and most families choose based on proximity, reputation, and available days.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Filling a new center to healthy occupancy typically takes 6 to 18 months. In-home programs fill faster because they need fewer children, but the ramp is still measured in months, not weeks.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad paid advertising outside your immediate area is largely wasted because families won't drive far. Early dollars are far better spent on tours, referral relationships, and a strong reputation than on regional ads.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, this is a full-time commitment from day one, not a side venture. An in-home program can support a comfortable owner income; a well-run center can support a strong income, but only at high occupancy.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible but requires a capable director and low staff turnover. Many owners step from teaching into oversight once a room runs smoothly without them, but full absentee ownership of a single small program is rare.
howItScales.sellable: Yes. Licensed centers with established enrollment, a clean inspection history, and a transferable lease sell to individuals and to childcare chains, often for a meaningful multiple of profit. License transferability rules vary by state and matter to buyers.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Strong directors, documented systems, reliable hiring pipelines, and capital. Each new location repeats the full licensing and buildout cost, so scaling is slow and capital-intensive, most growth comes from adding classrooms before adding sites.

strongFit: You have real experience with young children and the patience the work demands
You can commit full-time and weather 6 to 18 months of thin or negative cash flow
You are comfortable with heavy regulation, inspections, and detailed record-keeping
You can hire, train, and retain caring staff in a high-turnover field
poorFit: You want passive income or to avoid daily hands-on involvement
You are uncomfortable with strict regulation and the liability of caring for children
You have limited startup capital and no cushion for a long enrollment ramp
You dislike managing people, since payroll and staffing will dominate your time
askYourself: Have I read my state's actual childcare licensing requirements, not a general guide?
Can I survive a year or more before the business reliably pays me?
Am I prepared for the weight of being responsible for children's safety every single day?

faqs.question: Do I need a license to open a preschool?
faqs.answer: Almost always yes. Caring for unrelated children for pay is regulated in every U.S. state, with separate license types for in-home programs and centers. Requirements cover background checks, staff training, ratios, square footage, fire safety, and inspections. Operating without a license risks fines and immediate closure, so confirm your state's rules before spending money on a space.

faqs.question: How much can I really make running a preschool?
faqs.answer: It depends almost entirely on enrollment and ratios. A near-full in-home program often nets the owner $4,000 to $9,000 a month, while a well-enrolled center can yield $8,000 to $18,000 or more. But payroll is the dominant cost, and below about 80 percent occupancy many programs barely break even because you still must staff to legal ratios.

faqs.question: What are child-to-teacher ratios and why do they matter so much?
faqs.answer: States set the maximum number of children one adult may supervise, and the ratios get stricter for younger children, sometimes as low as 4 infants per adult. Ratios drive your staffing costs and cap how lean you can run. You must staff to ratio whether a classroom is full or half-empty, which is why occupancy is the key to profitability.

faqs.question: Is an in-home preschool cheaper than opening a center?
faqs.answer: Yes, considerably. An in-home licensed program uses your existing space, serves fewer children, and has lighter (though still real) requirements, so startup can be under $25,000. A center involves a commercial lease, code buildout, and more staff, pushing startup well into six figures. Many operators start in-home and only scale to a center after proving demand.

faqs.question: What is the biggest financial risk?
faqs.answer: Two things: a slow enrollment ramp that drains cash while fixed payroll continues, and a serious safety incident. Child injuries carry heavy liability and can damage your reputation overnight, which is why insurance, supervision, and documentation are non-negotiable rather than optional.

faqs.question: Can I accept government childcare subsidies?
faqs.answer: In most states yes, by enrolling as an approved provider for childcare assistance programs. Subsidies can fill seats and stabilize enrollment, but reimbursement rates are sometimes below private tuition and payments can lag. Many operators use a mix of private-pay and subsidized families to balance occupancy and cash flow.

faqs.question: How long before a new preschool is profitable?
faqs.answer: Realistically 6 to 18 months for a center to reach the occupancy needed to clear a profit, and several months even for an in-home program. Plan your finances around a long ramp; assuming you'll fill quickly is the most common way new operators run out of money.

faqs.question: What qualifications do I need to run a preschool?
faqs.answer: Requirements vary by state, but directors and lead teachers typically need early-childhood credentials, background clearances, CPR and first-aid certification, and minimum training hours. Even where the owner isn't required to be credentialed, real experience with young children is essential, this is not a business to learn on the job at children's expense.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Childcare Workers and Preschool Teachers occupational data
Child Care Aware of America, state licensing requirements and cost-of-care reports
National Association for the Education of Young Children (NAEYC), program standards and accreditation
Procare and Brightwheel operator resources, enrollment, tuition, and ratio benchmarks
State childcare licensing agency guidelines (vary by state) for ratios, fees, and inspections
